title: President Marcos' ICC options

audience_match: President Marcos and his foreign-policy advisers
DOJ and OSG officials deliberating cooperation policy
Civic-minded readers tracking the ICC question
Legal academics engaging with Pangilinan v. Cayetano obiter doctrine

primary_topics: Three Marcos options on the ICC investigation: (1) status quo, (2) rejoin the ICC, (3) cooperate without rejoining
ICC Appeals Chamber 3-2 decision (July 18, 2023) authorizing Khan to resume investigation
Constitutional question: can the President unilaterally recall the withdrawal letter without new Senate ratification?
VP Sara Duterte's formal request to DOJ not to cooperate with Khan and the ICC
sub_topics: Status quo: no rejoining; respect for the AC decision; SolGen Guevarra's policy that Khan investigators may enter PH 'in accordance with our laws' without cooperation
Majority of three in AC: agreed our government had not shown genuine willingness or ability to prosecute the 'brains' of the alleged crimes

Vocal minority of two (CJ agrees): ICC did not acquire jurisdiction â€” Bensouda's petition (May 24, 2021) and PTC grant (Sept 15, 2021) both fell outside the two-year reglementary period after PH withdrawal took effect March 17, 2019
Majority simply ignored the jurisdiction-by-period issue by treating it as waived
Khan's silence after the 3-2 ruling â€” has not publicly announced resumption
Rejoin option: President's plenary foreign-affairs power; withdrawal was unilateral so reversal can be unilateral; Senate Resolution No. 57 of Aug 23, 2011 still stands
Pangilinan v. Cayetano (March 16, 2021, J Leonen) obiter backing unilateral reversal with 'logic, reason, and wisdom'

Third option: cooperation without rejoining â€” open NBI, PNP, DOJ Chief Prosecutor facilities; possible outcomes (ICC concludes PH is genuinely willing/able, or ICC ensnares culprits via its own findings)
VP Sara Duterte's stance for status quo: 'upholding the self-respect and dignity of our country'

signature_phrases.phrase: Maintain the status quo, Rejoin the ICC, and Cooperate without rejoining the international tribunal
the vocal minority of two insisted, and I respectfully agree
it simply ignored this issue, saying that our government has waived its right to pose it
the President (regardless of who the incumbent is) may simply reverse his own act by recalling the letter of withdrawal without need of a new Senate ratification
rendered merely via an obiter dictum (side remark)
backed up this obiter with logic, reason, and wisdom
comity of nations and our abiding respect for the rule of law
ensnare the alleged perpetrators into the sticky ICC web
demonstrating the viability and capacity of our local institutions to render justice though the heavens may fall
Which option will the President take? Abangan.

people: Former ICC chief prosecutor Fatou Bensouda
Senior Associate Justice Marvic M.V.F. Leonen
cases: Pangilinan v. Cayetano (March 16, 2021, J Leonen) â€” obiter on Senate concurrence in treaty withdrawal
laws_treaties: Senate Resolution No. 57 (Aug 23, 2011) ratifying Rome Statute
Unilateral PH withdrawal from ICC (effective March 17, 2019)
events: Nov 1, 2011-March 16, 2019 â€” covered ICC investigation period
March 17, 2019 â€” PH withdrawal from ICC took effect
May 24, 2021 â€” Bensouda petition to PTC for preliminary examination authority
Sept 15, 2021 â€” PTC grant of preliminary examination
July 18, 2023 â€” ICC AC 3-2 decision authorizing Khan to resume
House of Representatives hearings on ICC question

stances.claim: President Marcos has three major options on the ICC question: maintain status quo (no rejoining but respect AC decision; Khan investigators may enter PH without government cooperation per SolGen Guevarra); rejoin the ICC (using plenary foreign-affairs power to recall the withdrawal letter); or cooperate without rejoining (open NBI, PNP, DOJ Chief Prosecutor facilities to Khan under comity of nations).
stances.rhetorical_move: three-option taxonomy framework for executive deliberation

stances.claim: The ICC Appeals Chamber's 3-2 majority of July 18, 2023 agreed only that the government had not shown genuine willingness or ability to prosecute the 'brains' of the alleged crimes, but did NOT rule on the more fundamental issue of whether the petition was filed within the undisputed two-year reglementary period after the March 17, 2019 withdrawal took effect â€” simply ignoring it by saying the government had waived its right to pose it.
stances.confidence: asserted with respectful agreement with the minority of two

stances.claim: Since PH withdrawal from the ICC was made unilaterally by the President without Senate concurrence, the President (regardless of incumbent) may simply reverse his own act by recalling the withdrawal letter without need of a new Senate ratification â€” the Senate's concurrence in Resolution No. 57 (Aug 23, 2011) was never reversed and 'still mightily stands.'
stances.rhetorical_move: symmetric-unilateral-action doctrine grounded in Pangilinan v. Cayetano obiter

stances.claim: The third option (cooperation without rejoining) can end in either of two seemingly disparate results â€” ICC investigators may conclude that PH is genuinely willing and able to investigate, OR they may rely on their own findings and ensnare the alleged perpetrators into 'the sticky ICC web' â€” and either outcome can be justified under the comity of nations and PH's respect for the rule of law.
stances.rhetorical_move: double-edged-outcome argument that frames cooperation as institutional self-test

notable_anecdotes.summary: Khan's revealing public silence â€” 'until now, and despite the 3-2 ruling of the AC, Khan has not publicly announced the resumption of his investigation' â€” used by CJ to suggest the unresolved reglementary-period issue may still be haunting the prosecutor.

VP Sara Duterte's formal request to the DOJ not to cooperate with Khan, framed by her as 'upholding the self-respect and dignity of our country and demonstrating the viability and capacity of our local institutions to render justice though the heavens may fall' â€” quoted as the political articulation of the status-quo option.
The closing one-word Tagalog cliffhanger: 'Abangan.' â€” a rare conversational tonal break in a doctrinal column.

decision_framework_signals: structures executive deliberation as three-option taxonomy rather than binary
uses reglementary-period jurisdiction-acquisition test as latent dispositive issue ignored by AC majority
applies symmetric-unilateral-action doctrine to treaty withdrawal/rejoinder
treats Senate concurrence as durable absent express Senate reversal
uses obiter dicta from PH Supreme Court as persuasive authority even where not binding
frames cooperation as institutional self-test with double-edged outcomes
target_audience: President Marcos and his foreign-policy advisers
DOJ and OSG officials deliberating cooperation policy
Civic-minded readers tracking the ICC question
Legal academics engaging with Pangilinan v. Cayetano obiter doctrine
register_markers: respectful-agreement-with-minority marker ('the vocal minority of two insisted, and I respectfully agree')
obiter-dictum doctrinal hedge ('rendered merely via an obiter dictum (side remark)')
humility marker ('My humble opinion is that...')
named-justice attribution as authority device ('fittingly penned by Senior Associate Justice Marvic M.V.F. Leonen')
vivid-metaphor register ('ensnare the alleged perpetrators into the sticky ICC web')
one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban maps President Marcos' three options on the ICC question prompted by recent House hearings: (1) maintain the status quo â€” no rejoining but respect for the July 18, 2023 ICC Appeals Chamber decision (3-2) authorizing Karim Khan to resume investigation of alleged crimes committed Nov 1, 2011 to March 16, 2019, with SolGen Guevarra's policy that Khan investigators may enter the country 'in accordance with our laws' but without government cooperation; (2) rejoin the ICC by recalling the withdrawal letter â€” feasible without new Senate ratification because the original withdrawal was unilateral and Senate Resolution No. 57 of Aug 23, 2011 was never reversed (Pangilinan v. Cayetano, March 16, 2021, J Leonen, backed this via obiter); or (3) cooperate without rejoining â€” opening NBI, PNP, and DOJ Chief Prosecutor facilities under comity of nations, an option that could end either in ICC concluding the Philippines is genuinely willing and able, or in ICC investigators ensnaring perpetrators 'into the sticky ICC web.' He respectfully agrees with the AC's vocal minority of two that the ICC may not have acquired jurisdiction because Bensouda's May 24, 2021 petition and the PTC's Sept 15, 2021 grant both fell outside the two-year reglementary period after the March 17, 2019 withdrawal â€” an issue the AC majority simply ignored by holding the government had waived it, which may explain Khan's continuing public silence. VP Sara Duterte has formally asked the DOJ not to cooperate, favoring the status quo. He closes with the Tagalog cliffhanger: 'Abangan.'
